summary: Gruber argues that Apple's dominance in technology design is partly a consequence of having no true rival that values design equally. He uses Stephen Fry's Guardian column as a springboard to observe that what appears as Apple fandom is really a hunger for great design that almost no other company satisfies. He draws an analogy to Canon and Nikon pushing each other to excellence, and to luxury car brands competing on design philosophy. He criticizes Microsoft's Zune for copying Apple's products rather than its design culture, and warns that companies dismissing Apple's success as a bubble may have it backwards — the aberration was Apple's weak 1990s, not its current dominance.
key_points: Devotion to Apple is often really devotion to great design, which almost no competitor offers
Canon and Nikon make each other better through rivalry; Apple lacks an equivalent design-focused competitor
Microsoft's Zune failed by copying Apple's products instead of copying Apple's culture and passion for original design
Nintendo is the only other tech company that shares Apple's emphasis on emotional, taste-driven design, but they don't compete directly
Apple itself would benefit from a true design rival, as competition prevents hubris and drives improvement
money_quotes: When you use a device every day, you cannot help, as a human being, but have an emotional relationship with it.
A grey box is not good enough, clunky and ugly is not good enough.
Canon's cameras are better because there's Nikon — and vice-versa.
If Apple is BMW, who is the Audi pushing them to be more elegant, or the Acura or Lexus pushing them to be more reliable?
Microsoft simply copied Apple's products rather than doing what they should have done — which is copy Apple's culture and passion for original design.
What if it's the other way around, and the aberration was Apple's tepid success in the 1990s?
A Nintendo mobile phone might kick some iPhone ass.
key_insight: Apple's lack of a design-focused rival isn't just bad for consumers — it's bad for Apple, because great design thrives on competition from peers who share the same values.
